content:
# Luke Chapter 24 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 119-120 | **Source Images:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_024_page_001.jpg, LUK_024_page_002.jpg

---

**1** But on the first day of the week, at the earliest dawn, they came to the tomb bringing the spices which they had prepared.

**2** And they found the stone rolled away from the tomb,

**3** but when they entered, they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus.

**4** And it came to pass, while they were perplexed about this, behold, two men stood beside them in dazzling garments.

**5** And as they became terrified and bowed their faces to the ground, the men said to them, "Why do you seek the living among the dead?

**6** He is not here, but has been raised. Remember how he spoke to you while he was still in Galilee,

**7** saying that the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and on the third day rise again."

**8** And they remembered his words.

**9** And returning from the tomb, they reported all these things to the Eleven and to all the rest.

**10** Now it was Mary Magdalene and Joanna and Mary the mother of James; and the other women with them were telling these things to the apostles.

**11** But these words appeared to them as idle talk, and they did not believe them.

**12** But Peter rose and ran to the tomb, and stooping down he saw the linen cloths alone; and he went away to his own home, marveling at what had happened.

**13** And behold, two of them on that same day were going to a village named Emmaus, which was sixty stadia from Jerusalem,

**14** and they were conversing with one another about all these things that had taken place.

**15** And it came to pass, while they were conversing and discussing together, Jesus himself drew near and was going with them,

**16** but their eyes were held so that they did not recognize him.

**17** And he said to them, "What are these words that you exchange with one another as you walk?" And they stood still, looking downcast.

**18** And one of them, named Cleopas, answered and said to him, "Are you the only one sojourning in Jerusalem who does not know the things that have happened in it in these days?"

**19** And he said to them, "What things?" And they said to him, "The things concerning Jesus the Nazarene, who was a man, a prophet mighty in deed and word before God and all the people,

**20** and how the chief priests and our rulers delivered him up to a sentence of death and crucified him.

**21** But we were hoping that he was the one about to redeem Israel. Indeed, besides all this, it is now the third day since these things happened.

**22** Moreover, some women from among us amazed us, having been at the tomb early in the morning,

**23** and not finding his body, they came saying that they had also seen a vision of angels who said that he is alive.

**24** And some of those with us went to the tomb and found it just as the women had said, but him they did not see."

**25** And he said to them, "O foolish ones and slow of heart to believe in all that the prophets spoke!

**26** Was it not necessary for the Christ to suffer these things and to enter into his glory?"

**27** And beginning from Moses and from all the Prophets, he interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.

**28** And they drew near to the village where they were going, and he acted as though he would go farther.

**29** But they urged him strongly, saying, "Stay with us, for it is toward evening and the day has already declined." And he went in to stay with them.

**30** And it came to pass, when he reclined at table with them, he took the bread and blessed it and broke it and gave it to them.

**31** And their eyes were opened, and they recognized him, and he became invisible from them.

**32** And they said to one another, "Was not our heart burning within us while he was speaking to us on the road, while he was opening the Scriptures to us?"

**33** And rising in that very hour, they returned to Jerusalem and found the Eleven gathered together and those with them,

**34** saying, "The Lord has truly been raised and has appeared to Simon!"

**35** And they were recounting the things on the road and how he was made known to them in the breaking of the bread.

**36** While they were saying these things, he himself stood in the midst of them and says to them, "Peace to you."

**37** But they were startled and became terrified, and supposed that they were seeing a spirit.

**38** And he said to them, "Why are you troubled, and why do doubts arise in your hearts?

**39** See my hands and my feet, that it is I myself. Touch me and see, for a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see that I have."

**40** And having said this, he showed them his hands and his feet.

**41** And while they still did not believe because of joy and were marveling, he said to them, "Have you anything to eat here?"

**42** And they gave him a piece of broiled fish,

**43** and taking it, he ate it before them.

**44** And he said to them, "These are my words which I spoke to you while I was still with you, that all things must be fulfilled which are written in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms concerning me."

**45** Then he opened their minds to understand the Scriptures,

**46** and said to them, "Thus it is written, that the Christ should suffer and rise from the dead on the third day,

**47** and that repentance for the forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all the nations, beginning from Jerusalem.

**48** You are witnesses of these things.

**49** And behold, I am sending the promise of my Father upon you. But remain in the city until you are clothed with power from on high."

**50** And he led them out as far as Bethany, and lifting up his hands, he blessed them.

**51** And it came to pass, while he was blessing them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven.

**52** And they, having worshiped him, returned to Jerusalem with great joy,

**53** and were continually in the temple, blessing God.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

Both manuscript pages (LUK_024_page_001.jpg and LUK_024_page_002.jpg) were examined. The folio displays the standard Sinaiticus four-column layout under the header ΛΥΚΑΝ. Page 119 spans from Luke 23:48 through approximately 24:17; page 120 spans approximately 24:23 through the ΚΑΤΑ ΛΥΚΑΝ subscription and into the opening of the Gospel of John.

All three translators agree on the following Sinaiticus-specific readings, confirmed against the manuscript images:

1. **v.3** — "of the Lord Jesus" (του κυριου Ιησου) is present. Western witnesses (D, some Old Latin) omit this. Sinaiticus retains it. All three translators concur; the consensus follows.

2. **v.6** — "He is not here, but has been raised" (ουκ εστιν ωδε αλλα ηγερθη) is present. Codex Bezae omits the latter clause. All three translators retain it; the consensus follows.

3. **v.12** — The verse is present in full. Western non-interpolation debates are noted but the Sinaiticus reading is clear. All three translators include it; the consensus follows.

4. **v.36** — "Peace to you" (ειρηνη υμιν) is present. Another Western non-interpolation candidate, but clearly legible. All three translators retain it; the consensus follows.

5. **v.40** — The verse is present in full. All three translators include it; the consensus follows.

6. **v.42** — "a piece of broiled fish" only, without the honeycomb addition. All three translators agree; the consensus follows.

7. **v.51** — "and was carried up into heaven" (και ανεφερετο εις τον ουρανον) is present. All three retain it; the consensus follows.

8. **v.52** — "having worshiped him" (προσκυνησαντες αυτον) is present. All three retain it; the consensus follows.

9. **v.53** — EMET reads "blessing God" (ευλογουντες τον θεον); LOGOS reads "blessing God"; PNEUMA reads "praising God" (as if αινουντες). EMET's detailed textual note explicitly identifies the Sinaiticus reading as ευλογουντες and notes that some manuscripts read αινουντες instead. The consensus follows the Sinaiticus reading: "blessing God." See Disagreement Log for details.

### Translation Principles Applied

**Governing principle:** Consilience — where all three translators agree, the shared reading carries maximum weight. Where they diverge, the consensus selects the rendering that best balances accuracy to the Greek, theological precision, and English clarity.

**Areas of strong agreement (all 53 verses):**
The three translations agree on the substance of every verse. The divergences are matters of English style and register rather than meaning. This high degree of convergence is itself a strong indicator that the Greek text has been accurately understood.

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **v.1 — "at the earliest dawn" vs. "at deep dawn" (EMET) vs. "at early dawn" (LOGOS) vs. "at the first light of dawn" (PNEUMA):** The Greek ορθρου βαθεως literally means "deep dawn." EMET's "at deep dawn" is most literal but unusual in English. LOGOS's "at early dawn" is natural but loses the depth. PNEUMA's phrase is evocative but expansive. The consensus selects "at the earliest dawn" — preserving the βαθεως (deep/early) emphasis while remaining natural English.

2. **v.4 — "dazzling garments":** EMET renders "clothing flashing-like-lightning"; LOGOS and PNEUMA both use variants of "dazzling/blazing garments." The consensus follows LOGOS's "dazzling garments" as the best balance of accuracy and naturalness for αστραπτουσαις.

3. **v.5 — "Why do you seek the living among the dead?":** All three are very close. EMET: "with the dead"; LOGOS: "among the dead"; PNEUMA: "among the dead for someone who is alive." The consensus follows the EMET/LOGOS wording with μετα rendered as "among" (LOGOS), which is more idiomatic than "with" while preserving the Greek structure.

4. **v.6 — "has been raised" vs. "has risen":** EMET and LOGOS both render ηγερθη as "has been raised" (divine passive). PNEUMA renders "has risen" (intransitive). The consensus follows EMET and LOGOS: the divine passive is theologically significant — God is the agent of resurrection. This is a key theological decision.

5. **v.11 — "idle talk" vs. "nonsense":** EMET: "idle-talk"; LOGOS: "idle talk"; PNEUMA: "nonsense." The Greek ληρος (hapax in NT) denotes babble or delirium. "Idle talk" preserves the specific register of the Greek; "nonsense" is more forceful but less precise. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

6. **v.12 — "marveling" vs. "stunned":** EMET and LOGOS render θαυμαζων as "marveling"; PNEUMA renders "stunned." While PNEUMA's reading captures the emotional state well, θαυμαζων fundamentally means "marveling/wondering" and does not yet carry the settled understanding that "stunned" implies. The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

7. **v.13 — "sixty stadia" vs. "about seven miles":** EMET preserves the original unit; LOGOS uses "sixty stadia"; PNEUMA converts to "about seven miles." The consensus retains "sixty stadia" as all three agree the Sinaiticus reading is εξηκοντα (not the variant 160 stadia). The original measurement is preserved, consistent with our literal-leaning formal register.

8. **v.17 — "looking downcast" vs. "looking-gloomy":** EMET: "looking-gloomy"; LOGOS: "looking downcast"; PNEUMA: "their faces heavy with grief." For σκυθρωποι, the consensus selects LOGOS's "looking downcast" — accurate and dignified.

9. **v.25 — "O foolish ones" vs. "How slow your hearts are":** EMET and LOGOS preserve ανοητοι as "O foolish ones." PNEUMA omits it, folding the rebuke into the "slow hearts" clause. The consensus preserves the direct address "O foolish ones" (following EMET/LOGOS), as ανοητοι is a distinct word in the Greek text and should not be elided.

10. **v.26 — "Christ" vs. "Messiah":** EMET and LOGOS render Χριστος as "Christ"; PNEUMA renders "Messiah." Since Χριστος functions as a title throughout Luke-Acts and "Christ" has become the standard English titular form, the consensus follows EMET/LOGOS consistently across vv. 26 and 46.

11. **v.29 — "Stay" vs. "Remain":** EMET: "Remain with us"; LOGOS: "Stay with us"; PNEUMA: "Stay with us." The consensus follows the majority with "Stay" — the more natural English imperative for μεινον in direct speech, while noting that the same root (μενω) carries deep Johannine resonance.

12. **v.31 — "he became invisible from them":** All three translators agree on the substance. EMET and LOGOS both render αφαντος εγενετο as "became invisible"; PNEUMA renders "vanished from their sight." The consensus retains "became invisible from them" — a striking and faithful rendering of this NT hapax.

13. **v.32 — "our heart" (singular):** All three preserve the Greek singular καρδια. This is theologically significant — the two disciples share one burning heart. The consensus preserves this.

14. **v.34 — "has been raised" vs. "has risen":** Same divine passive decision as v.6. EMET: "has been raised"; LOGOS: "has been raised"; PNEUMA: "has risen." Consensus: "has been raised."

15. **v.36 — Historical present "says":** EMET and LOGOS both preserve the historical present λεγει as "says." PNEUMA normalizes to "said." The consensus preserves the historical present — it creates immediacy at this dramatic moment and is faithful to the Sinaiticus text.

16. **v.37 — "spirit" vs. "ghost":** EMET and LOGOS render πνευμα as "spirit"; PNEUMA renders "ghost." In this context, the disciples are not thinking of the Holy Spirit but of an apparition; however, "spirit" is the more literal rendering of πνευμα and avoids the colloquial associations of "ghost." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

17. **v.49 — "behold" vs. "look":** EMET and LOGOS render ιδου as "behold"; PNEUMA renders "look." The consensus retains "behold" as the more formal rendering appropriate to Jesus' solemn commissioning speech.

18. **v.53 — "blessing" vs. "praising":** As noted in manuscript verification, EMET and LOGOS read ευλογουντες ("blessing"); PNEUMA reads αινουντες ("praising"). The Sinaiticus reading is ευλογουντες per EMET's detailed textual note. The consensus follows the manuscript: "blessing God." This creates a beautiful inclusio with v.50 where Jesus blesses the disciples — the Gospel ends with the community returning blessing to God.

### Structural Note

This chapter, the final chapter of Luke's Gospel, operates as a single sustained day — from dawn at the tomb to evening at Emmaus to night in Jerusalem. All three translators recognize this literary architecture. The consensus preserves the temporal markers that structure this arc: "at the earliest dawn" (v.1), "toward evening" (v.29), "in that very hour" (v.33).

Luke's Gospel began in the temple with Zechariah (1:8-9) and ends in the temple with the community "blessing God" (24:53). The consensus preserves "blessing" (ευλογουντες) rather than "praising" precisely because the same word-family (ευλογεω) appears in Jesus' blessing of the disciples in v.50 and throughout Luke's infancy narrative. This verbal thread is part of Luke's deliberate theological architecture.

### Acknowledgments

EMET provided the most rigorous textual apparatus, identifying 13 significant variants and preserving fine-grained grammatical detail. LOGOS provided the strongest balance of accuracy and readability, and the consensus draws most heavily from LOGOS's renderings. PNEUMA provided the deepest theological and literary insight, particularly on the Emmaus narrative's structure of presence-before-recognition (v.31 note) and the garment metaphor for the Spirit's power (v.49 note). The council's work converges remarkably on this chapter — a testament to the clarity of Luke's Greek and the power of his resurrection narrative.

---

*Produced by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, 2026-03-05.*
*The SOPHIA Bible — Consilience in the service of truth.*
*This is the final chapter of the Gospel According to Luke.*
